query: advantage of covering resection margin in distal pancreatectomy

retrieved_docs.title: Distal Pancreatectomy -- Contraindications -- Total Pancreatectomy
retrieved_docs.content: Lesions that are amenable for removal with sparing of pancreatic tissue by another resection type

retrieved_docs.title: Distal Pancreatectomy -- Contraindications -- Central Pancreatectomy
retrieved_docs.content: Pancreatic cancer (inability to remove all cancer and lymphatic involvement)

retrieved_docs.title: Distal Pancreatectomy -- Technique or Treatment -- Central Pancreatectomy
retrieved_docs.content: A central pancreatectomy allows the surgeon to conserve pancreatic parenchyma if the given lesion is operable with this technique. Central pancreatectomy allows much more conservation of the pancreas with postoperative long-term diabetes outcomes, only being 11 to 12% versus 20 to 50% of distal pancreatectomy patients. The exocrine function is also maintained at a much higher rate with central pancreatectomy, with only 10% of patients requiring supplementation versus 27% of distal pancreatectomy patients. Central pancreatectomy also preserves the entire biliary anatomy and avoids splenectomy. Central pancreatectomy is not indicated for pancreatic cancer as margins that remain may harbor malignancy, and lymph node harvest is not as sufficient. Surgical intervention for central pancreatectomy is only reserved for benign or potential borderline lesions and is thus probably the least likely utilized pancreatic resection surgery. The distal pancreas must have 5 to 6 cm of remaining parenchyma.

retrieved_docs.title: Distal Pancreatectomy -- Indications -- Central Pancreatectomy
retrieved_docs.content: Benign or borderline lesions located in the neck or proximal body of the pancreas Enucleation of the lesion unable to be performed at the neck/proximal body Trauma-related injury to the neck/proximal body Among other considerations will need a distal pancreatic tail >5 to 6 cm to make pancreatic conservation worth the risk [8]

retrieved_docs.title: Distal Pancreatectomy -- Contraindications -- Distal Pancreatectomy
retrieved_docs.content: Pancreatic lesions that are not able to be fully removed with a distal pancreatectomy or achieve an R0 resection.

retrieved_docs.title: Distal Pancreatectomy -- Technique or Treatment
retrieved_docs.content: After the entire pancreatic neck, body, and tail are exposed, the neck of the pancreas can be mobilized. The superior splenic vessels can remain intact and do not require ligation. Several of the small vessels running on the neck of the pancreas may need to be ligated for adequate mobilization of this area. The proximal portion of the pancreas can be transected using a stapler with a reinforced staple line. As with other pancreatic transections, the distal part of the pancreas can have two stay sutures placed to help control hemostasis before the transection occurs. The gland can then be transected with a scalpel or energy device. The margins are then sent immediately to pathology to confirm a negative margin. The distal remnant pancreas can then be drained either into the stomach or into the small bowel. If a pancreaticogastrostomy is the choice for drainage, then the pancreatic body must be mobilized off the splenic vessels for better mobilization to the stomach. The pancreas must be mobilized enough for the pancreas to be delivered into the stomach. This is done by making an anterior and posterior gastrotomy. The pancreatic remnant is then placed into the stomach without any added tension to either structure. The posterior stomach can then be sewn to the pancreatic parenchyma into the capsule with either 3-0 PDS or silk suture in a simple interrupted fashion or running technique. The anterior portion of the stomach is then closed in two layers with underlying PDS suture and silk for the outer layer. A drain is then left next to the newly created anastomosis. A jejunal limb can be used for the pancreaticojejunal anastomosis for the pancreatic remnant. The jejunum can be transected 15 to 20 cm distal to the ligament of Treitz. A limb of jejunum approximately 35 to 40 cm long can be passed through the mesocolon opening to lay beside the pancreatic remnant without any tension. The distal jejunum continuity can be restored with a jejunojejunostomy anastomosis. The pancreatic duct to jejunum anastomosis can be performed with an end to side anastomosis. The anastomosis can be created in two layers; the first layer is created by sewing the jejunal serosal layer to the pancreatic capsule in a running fashion. The pancreatic duct is sewn to the jejunum by first making a small enterotomy in the jejunum. The pancreatic duct is then sewn directly to the mucosa with either 5-0 PDS or vicryl. A small feeding tube can be placed through the jejunal wall and placed inside the pancreatic duct to help aid as a stent for the duct. The anterior pancreatic to jejunum anastomosis is then completed using a running 5-0 prolene suture. A drain is left near the anastomosis and pancreatic stump. The proximal pancreas can also be anastomosed to this portion of the jejunum if the stapled edge is felt to be too thick for a secure staple line or if it is thought this area may have a pancreatic duct stricture. This allows the head of the pancreas to drain and may save the patient from developing a pancreatic fistula if the stump were to break down. This anastomosis is performed in the manner as the previously created pancreaticojejunostomy. [13] [14] [15]

retrieved_docs.title: Distal Pancreatectomy -- Indications -- Distal Pancreatectomy
retrieved_docs.content: Benign or malignant tumors involving the body or tail of the pancreas located to the left side of the superior mesenteric vein Chronic pancreatitis confined to the body or tail Pseudocyst involving the tail of the pancreas Trauma to the distal pancreas Ductal disruption or stricture +/- pancreatic fistula in body or tail

retrieved_docs.title: Distal Pancreatectomy -- Contraindications -- Laparoscopic Technique
retrieved_docs.content: Severe obesity (surgeon experience level dependent) Previous major abdominal surgeries (adhesive disease) Advanced malignancy Involving vascular structures (surgeon experience level dependent) Unable to tolerate pneumoperitoneum

retrieved_docs.title: Distal Pancreatectomy -- Technique or Treatment -- Total Pancreatectomy
retrieved_docs.content: If pylorus preservation was done, the duodenojejunostomy could be placed 20 cm distal to the newly created biliary enteric anastomosis. If the standard technique was performed, then a gastrojejunostomy can be created 20 cm distal to the hepaticojejunal anastomosis using either stapled anastomosis or hand sewn with an inner layer of 3-0 PDS and outer layer of 3-0 silk suture. The anastomosis should all then be checked for leaking. A ray-tech can be placed around the posterior aspect of the biliary anastomosis to look for bile spillage. After confirming all anastomosis are intact and open, the decision can be made to place feeding access with either a gastrostomy tube or distal feeding jejunostomy tube. Closed suction Jackson pratt drains can then be placed in the areas of the newly created anastomosis with placement on the anterior abdominal wall. They can be sutured in place with 3-0 Nylon.

retrieved_docs.title: Distal Pancreatectomy -- Technique or Treatment -- Total Pancreatectomy
retrieved_docs.content: A total pancreatectomy requires exposure of the entire organ. The hepatic flexure is mobilized as needed to allow for a full kocherization of the duodenum extending to the aorta. This complete Kocher maneuver allows full visualization of the head of the pancreas with a direct view of the retroperitoneal attachments. The surgeon can then place their hand behind the head of the pancreas to palpate for any masses involving the superior mesenteric artery meaning that an R0 resection could not be obtained). The gastrohepatic ligament can then be dissected with full circumferential exposure of the common bile duct. The gallbladder can then be dissected from the fossa with separation from the liver bed. The cystic artery and cystic duct can then be fully dissected with clip ligation and division.

retrieved_docs.title: Pancreaticoduodenectomy (Whipple Procedure) -- Technique or Treatment -- Pancreatic Resection
retrieved_docs.content: Pancreatic division: Stay sutures are placed at the superior and inferior borders of the pancreas on either side of the planned transection line. The pancreas is then divided using sharp dissection or electrocautery, with bleeding controlled and the pancreatic duct margin sent for frozen section examination. The pancreatic head and uncinate process are carefully separated from the SMV, with small venous branches clipped or ligated. The final step involves dissecting the uncinate process from the SMV to the lateral border of the SMA. Once completed, the specimen is submitted for permanent pathology. Hemostasis is ensured, and preparation for reconstruction begins.

retrieved_docs.title: Distal Pancreatectomy -- Technique or Treatment -- Distal Pancreatectomy
retrieved_docs.content: When performing a distal pancreatectomy, the surgeon usually knows if this is being performed for a malignant process. If there is a malignant tumor of the distal pancreas, the spleen cannot be preserved. Suppose the resection is for a benign distal pancreatic lesion. The spleen can be preserved. We will discuss both techniques below. Distal Pancreatectomy with splenectomy - Once the pancreas has been exposed via entry into the lesser sac by dividing the gastrocolic ligament as previously discussed, the splenic flexure and colon can be further mobilized. The exposure can be from a lateral to medial approach with mobilization of the pancreas and spleen from the retroperitoneum and vasculature with transection of the pancreas, or it can be performed with a medial to lateral approach with transection of the pancreas at the confluence of the superior mesenteric vein and splenic vein and then complete the dissection laterally. Either technique can be used for distal pancreatectomy.

retrieved_docs.title: Distal Pancreatectomy -- Technique or Treatment -- Total Pancreatectomy
retrieved_docs.content: The superior and inferior pancreaticoduodenal arteries can then be identified, ligated, and divided. The pancreas can then be skeletonized from the superior mesenteric artery attachments. The pancreas can then be removed and passed off the table for marking of the margins and sending specimens from the bile duct, duodenal, and uncinate margins. After the margins are confirmed, reconstruction is performed. The biliary enteric anastomosis can then be created using the distal end of the jejunum. An end to side anastomosis is performed from the distal common hepatic duct to the proximal loop of the jejunum. This anastomosis is performed in one layer using interrupted 5-0 PDS suture. The ligated falciform ligament can be wrapped around the biliary anastomosis; depending on if the pylorus-preserving technique was performed or the standard technique, the proximal stomach anastomosis can be performed.

Review the management options available for a total, central, or distal pancreatectomy. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 2 -- Community 743
retrieved_docs.content: Exploration procedures constitute a broad category of surgical interventions that are employed to investigate, access, and treat pathology in diverse anatomical regions, and they are organized under the parent clinical actions “Clinical actions A – G.” Within this category, a partial or complete adrenalectomy—or exploration of the adrenal gland with or without biopsy—can be performed via transabdominal, lumbar, or dorsal approaches, and may also include excision of an adjacent retroperitoneal tumor. Cholecystectomy procedures that incorporate exploration of the common bile duct are represented in several variants: standard duct exploration, duct exploration with cholangiography, and duct exploration combined with transduodenal sphincterotomy or sphincteroplasty, with or without cholangiography; these can be executed laparoscopically. retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 1 -- Community 97
retrieved_docs.content: Exploration procedures constitute a broad surgical category that encompasses organ‑specific and region‑specific operative techniques used to investigate, diagnose, and treat pathology across multiple anatomical sites. Within this category, adrenalectomy (partial or total) may be performed via transabdominal, lumbar, or dorsal approaches and can include biopsy or excision of an adjacent retroperitoneal tumor. Cholecystectomy combined with common‑bile‑duct exploration includes standard duct exploration, cholangiography‑guided exploration, choledochoenterostomy, and transduodenal sphincterotomy or sphincteroplasty, with or without cholangiography; similar principles apply to choledochotomy or choledochostomy procedures that incorporate exploration, drainage, or calculus removal. Cranial exploration is represented by exploratory craniectomy or craniotomy, subdivided into infratentorial (posterior‑fossa) and supratentorial approaches, as well as suboccipital craniectomy for cranial‑nerve

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 28 -- Level 3 -- Community 1502
retrieved_docs.content: Excision is classified as a clinical action within the broader group “Clinical actions A‑G” and serves as an umbrella term for a wide range of operative procedures. It includes adrenalectomy—whether partial or complete, performed transabdominally, laparoscopically, or dorsally, with or without biopsy and with possible removal of adjacent retroperitoneal tumor—as well as several biliary surgeries such as cholecystectomy with exploration of the common duct, choledochoenterostomy, transduodenal sphincterotomy or sphincteroplasty, and associated cholangiography, which can be performed via open or laparoscopic techniques. Craniectomy or craniotomy performed exploratively in the infratentorial (posterior fossa) region also falls under Excision, as do various parathyroidectomy approaches—including cervical, sternal‑split, and transthoracic routes—each potentially combined with mediastinal exploration. In spinal surgery, Excision encompasses laminotomy (hemilaminectomy) with nerve‑root decompression, partial facetectomy, foraminotomy, and excision of herniated intervertebral disc at a single lumbar level, but explicitly excludes laminectomy procedures that involve only exploration or decompression of the spinal cord or cauda equina without facetectomy, foraminotomy, or discectomy for spinal stenosis across cervical and thoracic segments (both 1‑2 vertebral levels and more extensive segments). Additionally, Excision covers “other or therapeutic procedures on nose, mouth and pharynx” and is linked to plastic procedures on the nose, highlighting its relevance to both general surgical and specialized reconstructive interventions. This comprehensive definition clarifies the scope of Excision, distinguishing it from specific laminectomy techniques while integrating diverse surgical modalities under a single procedural category.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 30 -- Level 2 -- Community 624
retrieved_docs.content: Excision is presented as a broad surgical category that encompasses a variety of operative techniques across multiple organ systems. Specific procedures that are classified as methods of Excision include partial or complete adrenalectomy or adrenal gland exploration (performed transabdominally, lumbarly, or dorsally, with or without biopsy), arthroscopic removal of loose bodies from a joint, cholecystectomy with common‑duct exploration combined with transduodenal sphincterotomy or sphincteroplasty (with optional cholangiography), transcranial orbital exploration with lesion removal, nipple exploration with excision of the lactiferous duct, and several forms of parathyroid surgery such as standard parathyroidectomy, parathyroidectomy with mediastinal exploration via a cervical approach, and parathyroidectomy with mediastinal exploration via a transthoracic approach. Additional therapeutic applications of Excision extend to other or therapeutic procedures on the nose, mouth, and pharynx, as well as percutaneous nephrolithotomy. Conversely, certain spinal procedures are explicitly excluded from the Excision category. These excluded methods comprise laminectomy with exploration and/or decompression of the spinal cord or cauda equina—without facetectomy, foraminotomy, or discectomy—performed for spinal stenosis across various spinal regions (sacral, thoracic, cervical, and lumbar) and differing numbers of vertebral segments (one or two segments versus more than two segments). Excision is also linked, albeit in a non‑procedural context, as the answer to a specific colorectal‑cancer diagnostic program entry, and it holds a relationship to plastic procedures on the nose. Together, these connections illustrate that Excision functions as an overarching term for many targeted surgical removals while clearly distinguishing itself from certain decompressive spinal surgeries.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 14 -- Level 2 -- Community 1272
retrieved_docs.content: Exploration procedures constitute a broad category of surgical actions that fall under the clinical grouping “Clinical actions A – G.” This category includes a variety of organ‑specific exploratory operations performed with distinct techniques. In adrenal surgery, both partial and complete adrenalectomy or adrenal gland exploration—performed transabdominally, via a lumbar or dorsal approach—may be carried out with or without biopsy, and can optionally include removal of an adjacent retroperitoneal tumor. Biliary exploration is represented by cholecystectomy combined with common duct exploration, which may be accompanied by cholangiography, choledochoenterostomy, or other ductal interventions. Choledochotomy or choledochostomy procedures that involve ductal exploration, drainage, calculus removal, and may be paired with transduodenal sphincterotomy or sphincteroplasty are also classified as exploration procedures. retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 2 -- Community 1219
retrieved_docs.content: Exploration procedures are defined as a broad group of surgical actions (Clinical actions A–G) used to investigate, decompress, or remove pathology across multiple anatomic regions, and each named technique functions either as a primary “method” or as a “possibly‑included method” when combined with adjunctive steps. In the adrenal gland, partial or complete adrenalectomy—or adrenal gland exploration with or without biopsy—performed via transabdominal, lumbar, or dorsal approaches, and when coupled with excision of an adjacent retroperitoneal tumor, are classified as exploration methods. Biliary exploration includes cholecystectomy with common‑duct exploration performed with cholangiography, choledochoenterostomy, transduodenal sphincterotomy or sphincteroplasty (with or without cholangiography), as well as choledochotomy or choledochostomy with drainage, calculus removal, and optional sphincterotomy; laparoscopic cholecystectomy with common‑duct exploration is recorded similarly.

retrieved_docs.content: Exploration procedures constitute a broad surgical category within the “Clinical actions A‑G” hierarchy that encompasses operative techniques designed to investigate, decompress, or remove pathology across multiple anatomic regions. In endocrine surgery, partial or complete adrenalectomy—performed transabdominally, laparoscopically, or dorsally—and adrenal gland exploration (with or without biopsy) may be combined with excision of adjacent retroperitoneal tumors. Hepatobiliary exploration includes cholecystectomy with common‑duct exploration, cholangiography, choledochoenterostomy, transduodenal sphincterotomy or sphincteroplasty, choledochotomy or choledochostomy with drainage, calculus removal, and optional cholecystotomy. Neurosurgical exploration comprises infratentorial (posterior‑fossa) and supratentorial craniectomy or craniotomy, suboccipital craniectomy for cranial‑nerve decompression, and transcranial orbital exploration with lesion excision. retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 3 -- Community 1712
retrieved_docs.content: Excision serves as a broad parent category for a variety of surgical actions, including the set of procedures labeled “Clinical actions A‑G.” Within this overarching category, many specific operative methods are classified as forms of excision: cholecystectomy with common‑duct exploration (both open and laparoscopic), choledochotomy or choledochostomy with duct exploration, drainage or calculus removal (with or without cholecystotomy and with transduodenal sphincterotomy or sphincteroplasty), transcranial orbital exploration with lesion removal, cervical and lumbar laminotomy (hemilaminectomy) that decompress nerve roots and may involve partial facetectomy, foraminotomy, or disc excision, radical orchiectomy for tumor with abdominal exploration, and parathyroidectomy with mediastinal exploration performed via a cervical approach or a sternal split approach. At the same time, the excision category explicitly excludes several laminectomy techniques used for spinal stenosis: thoracic laminectomy with cord or cauda equina decompression without facetectomy, foraminotomy, or discectomy for one or two vertebral segments; and cervical, lumbar, or thoracic laminectomy for more than two vertebral segments performed under the same constraints. Excision is also linked to other therapeutic procedures involving the nose, mouth, and pharynx, and it has a relationship with plastic procedures on the nose, indicating that excisional techniques extend beyond deep organ surgery to include facial and upper aerodigestive tract interventions. This integrated view highlights excision as a versatile surgical concept that encompasses numerous specific operative methods while delineating clear boundaries for procedures it does not cover.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 3 -- Community 2115
retrieved_docs.content: Exploration procedures encompass a broad range of operative techniques in which surgeons directly inspect, access, or remove pathology from specific anatomic regions. The category includes adrenal gland surgery such as adrenalectomy—partial or complete, performed transabdominally, lumbarly, or dorsally, with or without biopsy and with excision of adjacent retroperitoneal tumor. Biliary exploration is represented by several cholecystectomy variants that incorporate common duct exploration, cholangiography, choledochoenterostomy, or transduodenal sphincterotomy/sphincteroplasty, each designed to evaluate and treat ductal stones or strictures. Cranial exploration is covered by both infratentorial (posterior fossa) and supratentorial craniectomy or craniotomy procedures, which allow direct visualization of intracranial lesions. Orbital pathology can be addressed through a transcranial approach to explore the orbit and excise lesions. Spinal exploration procedures include laminectomies—cervical, sacral, lumbar, thoracic, or multilevel—performed without facetectomy, foraminotomy, or discectomy to decompress the spinal cord or cauda equina in conditions such as spinal stenosis; these may be listed as core methods or as possibly included techniques depending on the extent of vertebral involvement. A related laminotomy (hemilaminectomy) technique provides nerve‑root decompression, partial facetectomy, foraminotomy, and removal of herniated disc material at a single lumbar level, often as a re‑exploration. Parathyroidectomy with mediastinal exploration via a sternal split approach exemplifies neck and mediastinal access for hyperparathyroidism. All of these specific methods fall under the broader “Exploration procedure” classification, which itself is organized under the parent category “Clinical actions A‑G.” This hierarchical structure clarifies how diverse surgical explorations are grouped for clinical documentation, coding, and patient‑care planning.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 3 -- Community 1713
retrieved_docs.content: Exploration procedures constitute a broad category of surgical interventions that are employed to examine, diagnose, or treat pathology within various anatomical regions. Specific operations that are classified as methods of exploration include partial or complete adrenalectomy—or adrenal gland exploration with or without biopsy—performed via transabdominal, lumbar, or dorsal approaches, and may also involve excision of adjacent retroperitoneal tumors. In hepatobiliary surgery, cholecystectomy combined with exploration of the common bile duct, whether accompanied by cholangiography, choledochoenterostomy, or transduodenal sphincterotomy/sphincteroplasty, is regarded as an exploration method. Neurosurgical exploration is represented by both infratentorial (posterior fossa) and supratentorial craniectomy or craniotomy performed for investigative purposes, as well as suboccipital craniectomy aimed at exploring or decompressing cranial nerves. Orthopedic exploration of the spinal canal is achieved through laminectomy with exploration and/or decompression of the spinal cord or cauda equina without facetectomy, foraminotomy, or discectomy; this technique is applied to one or two vertebral segments in the cervical, lumbar (excluding spondylolisthesis), and sacral regions, and may be listed as a possibly included method in broader procedural codes. Additional exploration procedures encompass nipple exploration with excision of the lactiferous duct, and parathyroidectomy or parathyroid exploration, which can be performed alone or with mediastinal exploration via sternal split or transthoracic approaches, including specific transthoracic mediastinal exploration. Collectively, these diverse surgical methods illustrate how exploration procedures are tailored to the target organ system and clinical objective, serving both diagnostic and therapeutic roles in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 2 -- Community 761
retrieved_docs.content: Exploration procedure is a broad surgical category that falls under the parent grouping “Clinical actions A – G” and comprises a wide array of specific operative techniques. It includes adrenalectomy, partial or complete, or exploration of the adrenal gland with or without biopsy performed via transabdominal, lumbar or dorsal approaches; cholecystectomy with exploration of the common duct and cholangiography; choledochotomy or choledochostomy with exploration, drainage or removal of calculi, which may be accompanied by transduodenal sphincterotomy or sphincteroplasty; exploratory craniectomy or craniotomy of the infratentorial (posterior fossa) region; exploration of the orbit by a transcranial approach with lesion removal; numerous laminectomy procedures that combine exploration and/or decompression of the spinal cord and/or cauda equina without facetectomy, foraminotomy or discectomy—ranging from 1‑2 vertebral segments in the cervical, lumbar (excluding spondylolisthesis) and thoracic spine to more than two segments in the cervical and thoracic spine; laminotomy (hemilaminectomy) with nerve‑root decompression, partial facetectomy, foraminotomy and/or excision of a herniated intervertebral disc in single cervical or lumbar interspaces; nipple exploration with excision of the lactiferous duct; and parathyroidectomy or exploration of the parathyroids, including variants with mediastinal exploration performed via cervical, sternal‑split or transthoracic approaches. Some of these methods are listed as possibly included within the exploration category, reflecting flexibility in how they may be combined or selected. Together, these procedures illustrate the diverse yet interrelated nature of exploratory surgeries used for diagnostic assessment and therapeutic intervention across multiple organ systems.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 2 -- Community 620
retrieved_docs.content: Exploration procedures constitute a broad category of surgical interventions that are employed to diagnose, assess, and treat pathology across multiple anatomical regions. In the endocrine system, adrenalectomy—performed partially or completely via transabdominal, lumbar, or dorsal approaches, with or without biopsy and occasionally combined with excision of adjacent retroperitoneal tumors—serves both therapeutic removal and exploratory evaluation of adrenal disease. Hepatobiliary exploration includes cholecystectomy with common duct exploration, which may be performed laparoscopically or with intra‑operative cholangiography, and choledochotomy or choledochostomy procedures that incorporate ductal drainage, calculus removal, and adjunctive transduodenal sphincterotomy or sphincteroplasty. Neurosurgical exploration is represented by suboccipital craniectomy for cranial nerve decompression and transcranial orbital exploration for lesion excision. Spinal exploration encompasses a spectrum of laminectomy techniques—cervical, lumbar, sacral, and thoracic—designed to decompress the spinal cord or cauda equina without facetectomy, foraminotomy, or discectomy, as well as more extensive multilevel thoracic laminectomies and laminotomies (hemilaminectomies) that include nerve‑root decompression, partial facetectomy, and disc excision. Additional specialized explorations include nipple exploration with lactiferous duct excision, parathyroidectomy with or without mediastinal exploration via sternal split or transthoracic approaches, and laparoscopic cholecystectomy with common duct exploration. Collectively, these methods illustrate how exploration procedures are tailored to the target organ and clinical objective, often integrating diagnostic biopsy, lesion removal, drainage, or decompression to optimize patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 4 -- Community 2757
retrieved_docs.content: as a possibly included method within a broader exploration procedure classification. In the abdominal and pelvic domains, radical orchiectomy for tumor removal can be performed with concurrent abdominal exploration, underscoring the integration of oncologic resection with intra‑abdominal assessment. Parathyroid surgery also exemplifies combined exploration: parathyroidectomy may be accompanied by mediastinal exploration using a cervical approach, a transthoracic approach, or a combined mediastinal and sternal‑split technique, reflecting the need to locate ectopic or mediastinal parathyroid tissue. Overall, the term “exploration procedure” unifies diverse surgical strategies that involve systematic inspection of target structures—whether biliary ducts, cranial compartments, spinal columns, or endocrine glands—often supplemented by adjunctive methods such as sphincterotomy, cholangiography, or mediastinal access. retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 4 -- Community 2756
retrieved_docs.content: Excision is presented as a broad surgical category that includes a wide variety of organ‑specific and region‑specific procedures while explicitly excluding certain extensive spinal decompressions. Within this category, adrenalectomy—whether partial or complete, performed transabdominally, lumbarly, or dorsally, and sometimes accompanied by removal of an adjacent retroperitoneal tumor—uses an excision method. Cholecystectomy combined with exploration of the common bile duct, either with cholangiography or with choledochoenterostomy, is also classified as an excision technique. Exploratory craniectomy or craniotomy of the infratentorial (posterior fossa) region falls under the same umbrella. In the spine, excision encompasses laminotomy (hemilaminectomy) with nerve‑root decompression, partial facetectomy, foraminotomy, and removal of herniated intervertebral disc when performed at a single interspace in the cervical or lumbar spine. By contrast, more extensive laminectomy procedures that decompress the spinal cord or cauda equina over more than two vertebral segments—whether cervical, lumbar, or thoracic—are specifically listed as excluded methods for excision. Other procedures grouped under excision include nipple exploration with lactiferous‑duct excision, parathyroidectomy or parathyroid exploration, and parathyroidectomy that involves mediastinal exploration via a sternal split approach. Excision is positioned within the higher‑level grouping “Clinical actions A‑G,” indicating that it is one of the clinical actions encompassed by that classification. Additionally, excision is linked to plastic procedures on the nose and is identified as a method within the broader set of “other or therapeutic procedures on nose; mouth and pharynx,” reflecting its relevance to reconstructive and therapeutic interventions in those anatomical regions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 3 -- Community 2577
retrieved_docs.content: Exploration procedures constitute a broad category of surgical interventions in which direct visual or operative assessment of an anatomic region is performed, often combined with therapeutic actions. In the context of adrenal surgery, both partial and complete adrenalectomy, as well as adrenal gland exploration with or without biopsy—performed via transabdominal, lumbar, or dorsal approaches and sometimes accompanied by excision of adjacent retroperitoneal tumors—are classified as exploration procedures. Biliary surgery includes cholecystectomy with exploration of the common duct, which may be performed alone or together with choledochoenterostomy; both are identified as methods of exploration. Similarly, choledochotomy or choledochostomy that involves duct exploration, drainage, calculus removal, and may be combined with transduodenal sphincterotomy or sphincteroplasty, represents an exploration procedure that can be a possibly included method. Neurosurgical exploration is
